Cloud notetakers promise not to train on your meetings. That is a promise about data they hold. QuickQuill holds none. The transcription and the summary both run on your Mac, so there is nothing to send and no one to trust. Switch on airplane mode and it all still works.

 On-device, end to endTranscription and summaries run entirely on your Mac. Neither makes a network request.
 No account, everThere is no sign-in and no profile. Nothing you record is tied to an identity, because there isn't one.
 Your recordings stay on your MacRecordings and transcripts are saved locally, kept for as long as you choose, and gone the instant you delete them.

results.title: QuickQuill: Private, on-device meeting notes for Mac
results.content:
Hi Product Hunt, I'm a solo developer in Japan👋 I built QuickQuill because I never got comfortable with meeting notetakers. You invite some company's bot into the call, it sits there in the participant list, everyone acts like that's normal, and the recording ends up on a server you never see. The apps say they won't look at it, and maybe they don't. I just didn't want work conversations to depend on that kind of trust. QuickQuill does everything on your Mac. It records both sides of the meeting, transcribes it, shows live subtitles with translation if you need them, and writes a summary when you stop. Nothing appears in the participant list because nothing joins the call. I recorded the demo on my M1 Mac with Wi-Fi turned off.  It requires macOS 26 on Apple Silicon. Dictation is free [...] Subscribe

Awesome when it works but beware if not. The meetings notes are great if everything works. Being able to customize the note-prompts is game-changing for me. However, the software is updated every week and it breaks every few weeks. Then the tool is not usable until the next update (for a few days or week), which is terrible. The support is a joke. ## The software just doesn't work
The software just doesn't work. 

It only processes the first few minutes of the meeting transcript then stops. [...] A new upgrade has been introduced (6/03/26). The face of the tool has totally changed. The option to upload a script taken from other devices is gone. e.g., smartphone. Uploading text scripts is no longer possible. Pre-selected reports could not be found. 

During a 72-minute offline committee meeting using a high-quality surround microphone, I noticed several issues:

The audio-to-text conversion was incomplete and inaccurate, leading to incorrect minutes and other reports. I had to use a third-party audio-to-text converter and then upload the results into Quill to get acceptable outcomes. Correcting the reports took several hours.

results.title: Quill Meetings Review: Private AI Notes, No Bots, No Cloud
results.content:
Quill processes everything on-device. Your audio never leaves your computer. The transcript never hits a cloud server. If you've ever had a security or compliance conversation with a CSO, that distinction matters enormously, and no major competitor can currently claim it. [...] I've been using it for three months. I haven't written a single meeting note in that time, and I'm capturing roughly 40% more action items than I was before.

## Why Local Processing Is the Real Differentiator#

Tools like Otter.ai and Fireflies transcribe on their servers. Your audio and transcript data leave your machine, get stored somewhere you don't control, and become subject to their retention and security policies. For most personal use that's fine. For anything involving clients, legal conversations, or internal strategy, it's a real problem. [...] Start HereSign in

Back to blog

AI Tools6 min read

# Quill Meetings Review: Private AI Notes, No Bots, No Cloud

Quill transcribes meetings locally on your device, no cloud storage, no bot joining your call, no subscription required. Here's how to set it up and get the most out of it.

Most AI meeting tools make you pick your poison: hand your audio to a cloud server, let a bot join your call where everyone can see it, or pay a subscription that's hard to justify for solo use. Quill doesn't make you choose. It transcribes locally on your device, stays invisible to other participants, and the free tier is genuinely unlimited, not a trial.

results.title: I Cancelled My Meeting-Notes Subscription.
results.content:
The whole tool is really just three steps glued together. Here’s each one, in the actual code that runs.

Step 1 — Capture what you hear _and_ what you say. A single `ffmpeg` line records from a combined audio device into a small 16 kHz mono file (the format speech models prefer):

ffmpeg -f avfoundation -i ":Meeting Capture" -ac 1 -ar 16000 meeting.m4a
`avfoundation` is macOS's audio system, `"Meeting Capture"` is the device that hears both your mic and the call, and `-ac 1 -ar 16000` keeps the file tiny. Nothing is sent anywhere — it's just written to disk.

Step 2 — Turn speech into text, on your GPU. This is the fast part. Whisper, compiled for Apple Silicon via MLX, runs right on the Mac’s graphics chip:

import mlx_whisper result = mlx_whisper.transcribe(

 "meeting.m4a", [...] ## How it works (the honest, 30-second version)

There’s no integration with Google Meet, Zoom, or Teams, and that’s deliberately a feature. Your Mac already _plays_ the meeting through its speakers and hears you through its mic. The tool simply captures that audio at the operating-system level — what you hear plus what you say — and runs it through two open-source models locally: Whisper turns speech into text on your Mac’s GPU, and a local LLM turns that transcript into clean notes. Because it never depends on the meeting app, the exact same setup works for Meet, Zoom, Teams, FaceTime, even a phone call on speaker. It doesn’t care which one you’re on. It just listens to your Mac.

## Install it — one line, once

Open Terminal, paste this, and press return: [...] The cloud is convenient. Owning the whole thing is better.

_The installer and full source are on__GitHub__— read it, fork it, break it. If this saved you an hour, drop a clap and follow; I publish a new “replace a paid tool with your own Mac” build every week. Next up: a private second brain that’s read every note and PDF you own — also free, also local._
